visual_notes: Alexandrite is unique — a single stone with two identities. Under northern daylight or fluorescent light it is distinctly green; under incandescent (candle, tungsten) light it turns vivid red-purple. The best Russian specimens shift from 'emerald' to 'ruby' — an impossible color change nicknamed 'the emerald by day, ruby by night' gem. No other natural gemstone produces such a dramatic and complete color change.
prompt_fragments: alexandrite: green under daylight, turning deep red-purple under incandescent bulb
Ural Mountains alexandrite — Tsar's gem shifting colors like Imperial Russia's uniform
emerald by day, ruby by night — the chromium paradox of chrysoberyl
vibe_notes: Alexandrite is the great paradox of gemology — one stone, two completely different identities, perfectly opposed. Green by day, red by night. The Russian gem named for a Tsar it would outlast by centuries. No other gem changes so completely.
symbolism: good luck and balanced opposing forces
folklore_summary: Alexandrite was officially discovered on April 17, 1834 — the 16th birthday of Crown Prince Alexander II of Russia, who would later become Tsar.
